rationale: AI-Scientist v2 performs literature search during ideation, not as a separate novelty gate. Treat this as provisional until a separate checker is added.

raw.Name: self_certified_guidance
raw.Title: Self-Certified Guidance: Confidence-Aware Classifier-Free Guidance for Diffusion Models
raw.Short Hypothesis: Classifier-free guidance uses a fixed global scale or a hand-crafted timestep schedule, implicitly assuming the conditional denoising direction is equally reliable across all samples and timesteps. We hypothesize this is false, and that many CFG failures arise when strong guidance amplifies unstable conditional directions at ambiguous or off-manifold states. A simple confidence signal computed from the denoiser’s own local self-consistency can identify these cases online. Using this signal to adapt guidance strength per sample and per timestep should improve the fidelity-diversity tradeoff, reduce oversaturation, and preserve mode coverage without requiring new labels, large auxiliary networks, or expensive retraining. This is the right setting because the error mechanism occurs during the denoising trajectory itself, so static schedules or post-hoc reranking cannot directly test or fix it.
raw.Related Work: The most relevant literature includes classifier guidance, classifier-free guidance, timestep-dependent guidance schedules, and guidance rescaling methods designed to reduce oversaturation or improve prompt adherence. Fast diffusion samplers and consistency-style models improve efficiency but generally do not treat guidance as a confidence-calibrated control problem. Some papers study uncertainty in diffusion models, but typically for inverse problems, posterior approximation, or ensemble estimation rather than online modulation of conditional guidance during unconditional generation. There is also related work on adaptive computation and mixture-of-experts denoisers, but these methods route computation rather than estimate whether the conditional direction itself should be trusted. Our proposal differs in a specific way: it derives a lightweight self-certification score from denoiser disagreement or local temporal consistency, then uses that score to modulate CFG at inference time. The contribution is not merely another timestep schedule, because the control signal varies across both samples and timesteps and is explicitly tied to denoiser reliability.
raw.Abstract: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) is one of the most effective tools for improving conditional diffusion models, yet it is usually applied with a fixed guidance scale or a simple timestep schedule. This assumes that the conditional denoising direction is uniformly reliable throughout sampling, an assumption that is unlikely to hold in ambiguous, low-density, or off-manifold regions of the diffusion trajectory. We propose Self-Certified Guidance (SCG), a simple inference-time method that estimates the reliability of the conditional update from the denoiser itself and uses this estimate to adapt guidance strength online. SCG computes a confidence score at each denoising step using local self-consistency signals such as disagreement under dropout, sensitivity to small latent perturbations, or temporal consistency across neighboring timesteps. Guidance is strengthened when the conditional direction is stable and weakened when it is unstable. Unlike prior guidance schedules, SCG is sample-dependent and requires no external classifier, no additional labels, and no large model changes. The method is easy to attach to standard diffusion samplers and can also be combined with guidance rescaling or fast samplers. We will evaluate SCG on class-conditional and lightweight text-conditional diffusion models using metrics covering fidelity, diversity, alignment, calibration, and compute. If successful, the work would introduce a practical and conceptually clean view of diffusion guidance as a confidence-aware control problem, yielding better samples and more robust behavior under strong guidance while remaining feasible for small academic labs.
Experiments: Implement SCG on a standard DDPM/ADM-style sampler. At each timestep compute a confidence score for the CFG direction d_t = eps_cond - eps_uncond using one of three cheap estimators: (1) dropout disagreement between two conditional forward passes, (2) latent perturbation sensitivity from two tiny perturbations of x_t with norm-matched correction, and (3) temporal consistency comparing d_t to the direction obtained after a provisional one-step denoise-and-return. Map confidence c_t to guidance scale s_t = s_min + (s_max - s_min) * c_t^alpha.

Benchmark on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-64 with public class-conditional checkpoints. Compare fixed CFG, simple timestep schedules, and guidance rescale baselines against SCG under equal function-evaluation budgets. Report FID, Inception Score, precision, recall, class accuracy from a pretrained classifier, and pairwise diversity statistics.

Measure whether SCG specifically improves the fidelity-diversity tradeoff at high guidance scales. Sweep maximum guidance strength and compare the Pareto frontier of FID versus recall or coverage. Test whether SCG preserves performance at mild scales and reduces collapse or oversharpening at aggressive scales.

Run a lightweight text-conditional experiment on a small latent diffusion setup or low-resolution text-to-image benchmark feasible for an academic lab. Compare CLIP score, FID, prompt-wise diversity, and human preference on prompts prone to CFG failures such as color intensity, repeated objects, and attribute binding.

Ablate the confidence estimator and mapping function: scalar confidence versus spatially averaged confidence, confidence computed from epsilon predictions versus x0 predictions, and monotone mappings such as linear, sigmoid, or thresholded adaptation. This identifies the simplest effective mechanism.

Test calibration directly: use confidence at early and middle timesteps to predict downstream failures such as low final CLIP score, classifier mismatch, or poor reconstruction consistency. Report rank correlation and AUROC to show whether self-certification is meaningful rather than merely a heuristic.

Evaluate compute-aware variants. Under a fixed average compute budget, use a single conditional pass when confidence is high and a double-check pass only when confidence is low. Compare to uniform two-pass baselines to test whether SCG can allocate inference compute adaptively.

Validate the mechanism on 2D toy mixtures and low-dimensional synthetic multimodal distributions where score fields are visualizable. Show that strong fixed guidance distorts trajectories in ambiguous regions and that SCG reduces this effect, improving mode coverage and making the phenomenon interpretable.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The idea may overlap partially with dynamic CFG scheduling or guidance rescaling literature, so careful empirical and conceptual differentiation will be necessary.
Confidence estimates based on dropout or perturbations may be noisy, and some architectures may not expose dropout at inference time.
Extra forward passes can reduce the efficiency advantage unless the confidence estimator is kept very cheap or used selectively.
Benefits may be concentrated in high-guidance regimes, which could narrow the practical impact if baseline models already perform best with moderate guidance.
If confidence is miscalibrated, SCG may weaken guidance when strong conditioning is actually needed, reducing alignment.

Small-scale text-to-image experiments may not fully predict behavior in large proprietary-scale models, though class-conditional and low-resolution results would still provide a strong conference paper if the mechanism is clear and reproducible.
